response:
To find the equilibrium population size for the first part we set the rate of change to zero meaning the population does not grow or shrink over time. The equation becomes zero equals r times P times one minus P over K minus h of t. However the harvesting function h of t varies with time so a true constant equilibrium does not exist in the strict sense unless we look for a quasi equilibrium or average state. If we assume the question seeks the population size that balances the average harvest we use the average value of the sine term which is zero. Thus the average harvest is ten turtles per year. We solve zero equals 0.1 times P times one minus P over 1000 minus ten. This simplifies to 0.1P minus 0.0001P squared equals ten. Rearranging gives 0.0001P squared minus 0.1P plus ten equals zero. Multiplying by 10000 yields P squared minus 1000P plus 100000 equals zero. Using the quadratic formula we find P equals one thousand plus or minus the square root of one million minus four hundred thousand divided by two. This results in two possible values approximately 1127 and 887. Since the carrying capacity is 1000 the only feasible equilibrium is 887 turtles.

For the second part we introduce a time dependent carrying capacity K of t equals 1000 minus 3t. The new equilibrium condition requires the growth rate to match the harvest rate at any given moment. We set zero equals r times P times one minus P over K of t minus h of t. This creates a time dependent equilibrium P sub e of t. As time passes the carrying capacity shrinks linearly while the harvest fluctuates. The population becomes unsustainable when the maximum possible growth cannot offset the minimum harvest or when the carrying capacity drops so low that no positive population can exist. Specifically we look for the time when the equilibrium value drops to zero or below. The critical point occurs when the carrying capacity is so small that even with zero harvest the population cannot sustain itself or when the required P to balance the equation becomes negative. In the worst case scenario where harvest is at its minimum of five turtles we solve for when the quadratic has no positive real roots. However a simpler threshold for total collapse is when the carrying capacity itself approaches the point where the intrinsic growth cannot support the minimum loss.
